entry.title: MAD-HTLC
entry.summary:
The discussion revolves around specific technical aspects and decisions made during the construction of cryptocurrency protocols, particularly focusing on the use of base fees in the MAD-HTLC model. It is highlighted that although the base fee was considered minimal and generally omitted from formal calculations, this decision may have warranted more explicit clarification in the assumptions section of the related document. The discourse also touches upon user experience (UX) considerations, discussing the balance between increasing fees or adjusting the `to_self_delay` parameter as a means of managing risks associated with channel abandonment scenarios.
Furthermore, the conversation includes a critical reflection on the incorporation of the Poon-Dryja channel construction within the paper. This method is described using an analogy to the knowledge of a preimage of a hash being tantamount to knowing the private key of the revocation public key, a concept reportedly easier to comprehend as explained in Patrick McCorry's Ph.D. thesis, which can be accessed [here](https://nms.kcl.ac.uk/patrick.mccorry/thesis.pdf). The email acknowledges that while the paper mainly addresses timelocked transactions vulnerable to bribery attacks, it perhaps inadequately sets the context for discussing other relevant constructions such as Tier Nolan's Atomic Swap and potentially overlooks alternative approaches like Spilman's one-way channels or Decker-Wattenhofer duplex channels. Moreover, there’s an admission that not addressing Eltoo in the paper was a significant omission.
Additionally, the email references various concepts and constructions not covered in the paper, including advanced atomic swaps proposed by Somsen or Fournier and Kanzure's vault proposal. It suggests that contexts requiring watchtowers might inherently be susceptible to timelocked bribes, indicating a broader application and implication of the discussed vulnerabilities beyond the specific cases mentioned. The correspondence closes with gratitude for the detailed analysis provided, reflecting a collaborative approach to refining and understanding these complex cryptographic mechanisms.
